name: Release

on:
  push:
    tags:
      - "v*"

permissions:
  contents: write

jobs:
  release:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      - uses: actions/setup-go@v5
        with:
          go-version-file: go.mod

      - name: Run GoReleaser
        uses: goreleaser/goreleaser-action@v6
        with:
          version: latest
          args: release --clean
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.RELEASE_TOKEN }}

file.content:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# Nurge installer
# Usage:
#   curl -fsSL https://install.nurge.sh | bash
#   curl -fsSL https://install.nurge.sh | bash -s -- --pre
#   curl -fsSL https://install.nurge.sh | bash -s -- v0.1.0

REPO="usenurge/nurge"
INSTALL_DIR="/usr/local/bin"
BINARY="nurge"

# --- Helpers ---

info()  { printf "\033[1;34m→\033[0m %s\n" "$1"; }
ok()    { printf "\033[1;32m✓\033[0m %s\n" "$1"; }
warn()  { printf "\033[1;33m!\033[0m %s\n" "$1"; }
fail()  { printf "\033[1;31m✗\033[0m %s\n" "$1" >&2; exit 1; }

# --- Detect platform ---

detect_os() {
  case "$(uname -s)" in
    Linux*)  echo "linux" ;;
    Darwin*) echo "darwin" ;;
    *)       fail "Unsupported OS: $(uname -s)" ;;
  esac
}

detect_arch() {
  case "$(uname -m)" in
    x86_64|amd64)  echo "amd64" ;;
    arm64|aarch64) echo "arm64" ;;
    *)             fail "Unsupported architecture: $(uname -m)" ;;
  esac
}

# --- Resolve version ---

resolve_version() {
  local requested="${1:-}"
  local pre="${2:-false}"

  if [ -n "$requested" ] && [ "$requested" != "--pre" ]; then
    echo "$requested"
    return
  fi

  info "Fetching latest release..."
  local releases
  releases=$(curl -fsSL "https://api.github.com/repos/${REPO}/releases?per_page=20") || fail "Failed to fetch releases"

  local version=""
  if [ "$pre" = "true" ]; then
    version=$(echo "$releases" | grep -o '"tag_name": *"[^"]*"' | head -1 | sed 's/.*"tag_name": *"//;s/"//')
  else
    version=$(echo "$releases" | grep -o '"tag_name": *"[^"]*"' | grep -v 'alpha\|beta\|rc' | head -1 | sed 's/.*"tag_name": *"//;s/"//')
  fi

  [ -z "$version" ] && fail "No release found"
  echo "$version"
}

# --- Download & install ---

install() {
  local version="$1"
  local os="$2"
  local arch="$3"

  local archive_name="${BINARY}_${os}_${arch}"
  local ext="tar.gz"
  [ "$os" = "windows" ] && ext="zip"

  local url="https://github.com/${REPO}/releases/download/${version}/${archive_name}.${ext}"

  info "Downloading ${BINARY} ${version} (${os}/${arch})..."
  local tmpdir
  tmpdir=$(mktemp -d)
  trap 'rm -rf "$tmpdir"' EXIT

  curl -fsSL "$url" -o "${tmpdir}/archive.${ext}" || fail "Download failed. Check that ${version} exists at https://github.com/${REPO}/releases"

  info "Extracting..."
  if [ "$ext" = "tar.gz" ]; then
    tar -xzf "${tmpdir}/archive.${ext}" -C "$tmpdir"
  else
    unzip -q "${tmpdir}/archive.${ext}" -d "$tmpdir"
  fi

  # Find the binary (may be nested in a directory)
  local bin_path
  bin_path=$(find "$tmpdir" -name "$BINARY" -type f | head -1)
  [ -z "$bin_path" ] && fail "Binary not found in archive"

  chmod +x "$bin_path"

  # Install to target directory
  if [ -w "$INSTALL_DIR" ]; then
    mv "$bin_path" "${INSTALL_DIR}/${BINARY}"
  else
    info "Need sudo to install to ${INSTALL_DIR}"
    sudo mv "$bin_path" "${INSTALL_DIR}/${BINARY}"
  fi

  ok "Installed ${BINARY} ${version} to ${INSTALL_DIR}/${BINARY}"
}

# --- Main ---

main() {
  local arg="${1:-}"
  local pre=false

  if [ "$arg" = "--pre" ]; then
    pre=true
    arg=""
  fi

  local os arch version
  os=$(detect_os)
  arch=$(detect_arch)
  version=$(resolve_version "$arg" "$pre")

  install "$version" "$os" "$arch"

  # Post-install: next steps
  echo ""
  echo "  Get started:"
  echo "    nurge my-outbound"
  echo "    cd my-outbound"
  echo ""
}

main "$@"

content.text:
No, there's no auto-update mechanism. Currently:

- **Install** is manual: `curl -fsSL https://install.nurge.sh | bash`
- **Version** is baked in at build time via ldflags (`buildVersion`, `buildCommit`, `buildDate`)
- **No version check** happens at startup or session start — there's nothing that compares the local version against the latest GitHub release

Users have to manually re-run the install script to get a new version.

Want me to build an auto-update check? The typical pattern would be:
1. On session start (or periodically), check `https://api.github.com/repos/usenurge/nurge/releases/latest` 
2. Compare against `buildVersion`
3. If newer exists, print a one-liner like `→ nurge v0.2.0 available. Run: curl -fsSL https://install.nurge.sh | bash`
4. Don't block — just inform and continue

Base directory for this skill: /Users/rejoesudarsan/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/superpowers/5.0.5/skills/brainstorming

# Brainstorming Ideas Into Designs

Help turn ideas into fully formed designs and specs through natural collaborative dialogue.

Start by understanding the current project context, then ask questions one at a time to refine the idea. Once you understand what you're building, present the design and get user approval.

<HARD-GATE>
Do NOT invoke any implementation skill, write any code, scaffold any project, or take any implementation action until you have presented a design and the user has approved it. This applies to EVERY project regardless of perceived simplicity.
</HARD-GATE>

## Anti-Pattern: "This Is Too Simple To Need A Design"

Every project goes through this process. A todo list, a single-function utility, a config change — all of them. "Simple" projects are where unexamined assumptions cause the most wasted work. The design can be short (a few sentences for truly simple projects), but you MUST present it and get approval.

## Checklist

You MUST create a task for each of these items and complete them in order:

1. **Explore project context** — check files, docs, recent commits
2. **Offer visual companion** (if topic will involve visual questions) — this is its own message, not combined with a clarifying question. See the Visual Companion section below.
3. **Ask clarifying questions** — one at a time, understand purpose/constraints/success criteria
4. **Propose 2-3 approaches** — with trade-offs and your recommendation
5. **Present design** — in sections scaled to their complexity, get user approval after each section
6. **Write design doc** — save to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md` and commit
7. **Spec review loop** — dispatch spec-document-reviewer subagent with precisely crafted review context (never your session history); fix issues and re-dispatch until approved (max 3 iterations, then surface to human)
8. **User reviews written spec** — ask user to review the spec file before proceeding
9. **Transition to implementation** — invoke writing-plans skill to create implementation plan

## Process Flow

```dot
digraph brainstorming {
    "Explore project context" [shape=box];
    "Visual questions ahead?" [shape=diamond];
    "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" [shape=box];
    "Ask clarifying questions" [shape=box];
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" [shape=box];
    "Present design sections" [shape=box];
    "User approves design?" [shape=diamond];
    "Write design doc" [shape=box];
    "Spec review loop" [shape=box];
    "Spec review passed?" [shape=diamond];
    "User reviews spec?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke writing-plans skill" [shape=doublecircle];

    "Explore project context" -> "Visual questions ahead?";
    "Visual questions ahead?" -> "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" [label="yes"];
    "Visual questions ahead?" -> "Ask clarifying questions" [label="no"];
    "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" -> "Ask clarifying questions";
    "Ask clarifying questions" -> "Propose 2-3 approaches";
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" -> "Present design sections";
    "Present design sections" -> "User approves design?";
    "User approves design?" -> "Present design sections" [label="no, revise"];
    "User approves design?" -> "Write design doc" [label="yes"];
    "Write design doc" -> "Spec review loop";
    "Spec review loop" -> "Spec review passed?";
    "Spec review passed?" -> "Spec review loop" [label="issues found,\nfix and re-dispatch"];
    "Spec review passed?" -> "User reviews spec?" [label="approved"];
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Write design doc" [label="changes requested"];
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Invoke writing-plans skill" [label="approved"];
}
```

**The terminal state is invoking writing-plans.** Do NOT invoke frontend-design, mcp-builder, or any other implementation skill. The ONLY skill you invoke after brainstorming is writing-plans.

## The Process

**Understanding the idea:**

- Check out the current project state first (files, docs, recent commits)
- Before asking detailed questions, assess scope: if the request describes multiple independent subsystems (e.g., "build a platform with chat, file storage, billing, and analytics"), flag this immediately. Don't spend questions refining details of a project that needs to be decomposed first.
- If the project is too large for a single spec, help the user decompose into sub-projects: what are the independent pieces, how do they relate, what order should they be built? Then brainstorm the first sub-project through the normal design flow. Each sub-project gets its own spec → plan → implementation cycle.
- For appropriately-scoped projects, ask questions one at a time to refine the idea
- Prefer multiple choice questions when possible, but open-ended is fine too
- Only one question per message - if a topic needs more exploration, break it into multiple questions
- Focus on understanding: purpose, constraints, success criteria

**Exploring approaches:**

- Propose 2-3 different approaches with trade-offs
- Present options conversationally with your recommendation and reasoning
- Lead with your recommended option and explain why

**Presenting the design:**

- Once you believe you understand what you're building, present the design
- Scale each section to its complexity: a few sentences if straightforward, up to 200-300 words if nuanced
- Ask after each section whether it looks right so far
- Cover: architecture, components, data flow, error handling, testing
- Be ready to go back and clarify if something doesn't make sense

**Design for isolation and clarity:**

- Break the system into smaller units that each have one clear purpose, communicate through well-defined interfaces, and can be understood and tested independently
- For each unit, you should be able to answer: what does it do, how do you use it, and what does it depend on?
- Can someone understand what a unit does without reading its internals? Can you change the internals without breaking consumers? If not, the boundaries need work.
- Smaller, well-bounded units are also easier for you to work with - you reason better about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more reliable when files are focused. When a file grows large, that's often a signal that it's doing too much.

**Working in existing codebases:**

- Explore the current structure before proposing changes. Follow existing patterns.
- Where existing code has problems that affect the work (e.g., a file that's grown too large, unclear boundaries, tangled responsibilities), include targeted improvements as part of the design - the way a good developer improves code they're working in.
- Don't propose unrelated refactoring. Stay focused on what serves the current goal.

## After the Design

**Documentation:**

- Write the validated design (spec) to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md`
  - (User preferences for spec location override this default)
- Use elements-of-style:writing-clearly-and-concisely skill if available
- Commit the design document to git

**Spec Review Loop:**
After writing the spec document:

1. Dispatch spec-document-reviewer subagent (see spec-document-reviewer-prompt.md)
2. If Issues Found: fix, re-dispatch, repeat until Approved
3. If loop exceeds 3 iterations, surface to human for guidance

**User Review Gate:**
After the spec review loop passes, ask the user to review the written spec before proceeding:

> "Spec written and committed to `<path>`. Please review it and let me know if you want to make any changes before we start writing out the implementation plan."

Wait for the user's response. If they request changes, make them and re-run the spec review loop. Only proceed once the user approves.

**Implementation:**

- Invoke the writing-plans skill to create a detailed implementation plan
- Do NOT invoke any other skill. writing-plans is the next step.

## Key Principles

- **One question at a time** - Don't overwhelm with multiple questions
- **Multiple choice preferred** - Easier to answer than open-ended when possible
- **YAGNI ruthlessly** - Remove unnecessary features from all designs
- **Explore alternatives** - Always propose 2-3 approaches before settling
- **Incremental validation** - Present design, get approval before moving on
- **Be flexible** - Go back and clarify when something doesn't make sense

## Visual Companion

A browser-based companion for showing mockups, diagrams, and visual options during brainstorming. Available as a tool — not a mode. Accepting the companion means it's available for questions that benefit from visual treatment; it does NOT mean every question goes through the browser.

**Offering the companion:** When you anticipate that upcoming questions will involve visual content (mockups, layouts, diagrams), offer it once for consent:
> "Some of what we're working on might be easier to explain if I can show it to you in a web browser. I can put together mockups, diagrams, comparisons, and other visuals as we go. This feature is still new and can be token-intensive. Want to try it? (Requires opening a local URL)"

**This offer MUST be its own message.** Do not combine it with clarifying questions, context summaries, or any other content. The message should contain ONLY the offer above and nothing else. Wait for the user's response before continuing. If they decline, proceed with text-only brainstorming.

**Per-question decision:** Even after the user accepts, decide FOR EACH QUESTION whether to use the browser or the terminal. The test: **would the user understand this better by seeing it than reading it?**

- **Use the browser** for content that IS visual — mockups, wireframes, layout comparisons, architecture diagrams, side-by-side visual designs
- **Use the terminal** for content that is text — requirements questions, conceptual choices, tradeoff lists, A/B/C/D text options, scope decisions

A question about a UI topic is not automatically a visual question. "What does personality mean in this context?" is a conceptual question — use the terminal. "Which wizard layout works better?" is a visual question — use the browser.

If they agree to the companion, read the detailed guide before proceeding:
`skills/brainstorming/visual-companion.md`

content.text:
Good, I've got the full picture. Here's what I see:

- **Releases** go to `usenurge/nurge` (public repo) via GoReleaser
- **Version** is stamped via ldflags (`main.version`, `main.commit`, `main.date`)
- **Install** is `curl | bash` downloading from GitHub Releases
- **No update check** exists anywhere

Before I design this, one question:

**Should the update actually self-replace the binary, or just notify the user?**

Options:
- **A) Notify only** — print "nurge v0.2.0 available, run `curl -fsSL https://install.nurge.sh | bash` to update" on next command. Zero risk, simple.
- **B) `nurge update`** — a subcommand that downloads and replaces the binary in-place (like `gh upgrade`). More convenient but needs sudo handling.
- **C) Both** — notify on session start, offer `nurge update` to act on it.

I'd recommend **C** — the notify is cheap and the update command is what users actually want. GTM users won't remember the curl command.

Good. Next question:

**How aggressive should the update check be?**

- **A) Every command** — check GitHub API on every `nurge` invocation. Adds ~200ms latency. Always fresh.
- **B) Once per day** — cache the check result in `~/.nurge/update-check.json` with a timestamp. Zero latency on subsequent runs that day.
- **C) Once per session** — check on first command in a Claude Code session (or terminal session), skip after that.

I'd recommend **B** — once per day. No perceptible slowdown for most usage, and GTM users running multiple commands in a session won't get hammered with network calls. The check itself runs in the background so it doesn't block the command.

content.text:
Makes sense. One more question then I'll present the design:

**For `nurge update`, should it self-replace the running binary directly, or re-run the install script?**

- **A) Self-replace** — download the right archive from GitHub Releases, extract, replace binary in-place. Fully self-contained, no curl dependency. Needs to detect OS/arch and handle permissions.
- **B) Shell out to install script** — run `curl -fsSL https://install.nurge.sh | bash` under the hood. Simpler, but depends on curl and the worker being up.

I'd recommend **A** — self-replace. It's more reliable, works offline-ish (only needs GitHub API), and you already have all the OS/arch/archive logic in install.sh that we can port to Go. Plus it avoids the "install script changed but binary didn't" desync.

Here's the design:

## Auto-Update: Architecture

**Two components:**

### 1. Update Checker (`internal/update/`)
- **Check function**: hits `https://api.github.com/repos/usenurge/nurge/releases/latest`, extracts tag name
- **Semver compare**: strips `v` prefix, compares against `buildVersion`. Skips if current version is `dev` (local build)
- **Cache**: writes result to `~/.nurge/update-check.json` (`{version, checked_at, download_url}`)
- **TTL**: 1 hour — "once per session" without needing to define sessions
- **Async**: runs in a goroutine during `Execute()` with 2s timeout. If it doesn't complete in time, silently skips — cache populates for next run
- **Notification**: after command output, prints `→ nurge v0.2.0 available. Run: nurge update`
- **Skipped for**: `update`, `version`, and when `buildVersion == "dev"`

### 2. `nurge update` command (`cmd/update.go`)
- Fetches latest release from GitHub API (or uses cached info)
- Detects OS (`runtime.GOOS`) and arch (`runtime.GOARCH`)
- Downloads the right `nurge_{version}_{os}_{arch}.tar.gz` archive
- Extracts to temp dir, finds binary
- Replaces current executable in-place (`os.Executable()` to find path)
- If permission denied, suggests `sudo nurge update`
- Prints `✓ Updated nurge to v0.2.0`
- Supports `--pre` flag for prerelease versions

### Data flow
```
Every command → goroutine checks cache → stale? → GitHub API → write cache
                                       → fresh? → has update? → print notification after output

nurge update → GitHub API → detect OS/arch → download archive → extract → replace binary
```

### File structure
```
internal/update/
  check.go    — CheckForUpdate, cache read/write, semver compare
  update.go   — SelfUpdate (download, extract, replace)
cmd/update.go  — cobra command wiring
```

Does this look right? Anything you'd change?

message.content: so if we build better way of adding skills in new update and the previou users already built their skillsm, how do they work?

content.text:
Good question — this is about **post-update migrations**, not just the binary update.

The issue: user updates nurge, but their `.claude/skills/nurge-*/SKILL.md` files were generated by the old version. New skill templates might have better structure, new fields, or entirely new skills.

**Two options:**

**A) Auto-migrate on next run** — after update, `nurge` detects skill template version < binary version, regenerates skills from current data + learnings. Old versions preserved in DB for rollback.

**B) Prompt via Claude** — after update, the next Claude Code session sees a notification: "Nurge was updated to v0.2.0. Run `/nurge:upgrade-skills` to regenerate your skills with the latest templates." Claude handles the regeneration conversationally, can explain what changed.

I'd lean **B** — because:
- Skills are personalized from user data + learnings. Blind regeneration might lose nuance Claude added
- Users (GTM people) should see what's changing, not have things silently rewritten
- Claude can merge: keep the personalized content, adopt the new structure
- Fits the "Go = dumb, Claude = smart" principle

The update check notification could include this: 
```
→ nurge v0.2.0 installed. Skills were updated — run /nurge:upgrade-skills to apply.
```

Want me to include this post-update skill migration in the design, or keep it separate and just build the auto-update first?

Good. Here's the final design with the skill migration piece added:

## Final Design

### 1. Update Checker (`internal/update/check.go`)
- GitHub API call to `repos/usenurge/nurge/releases/latest`
- Semver compare against `buildVersion` (skip if `dev`)
- Cache in `~/.nurge/update-check.json`, 1hr TTL
- Async goroutine in `Execute()`, 2s timeout
- Notification: `→ nurge v0.2.0 available. Run: nurge update`

### 2. Self-Update (`internal/update/update.go` + `cmd/update.go`)
- Detect OS/arch via `runtime`
- Download + extract correct archive from GitHub Releases
- Replace binary at `os.Executable()` path
- `--pre` flag for prereleases
- Suggest `sudo` on permission error

### 3. Post-Update Skill Migration
- After successful update, write `.nurge/pending-skill-upgrade.json` with `{from_version, to_version, updated_at}`
- On next `nurge` command that reads DB, detect this marker and print: `→ Skills templates updated. Run /nurge:upgrade-skills in Claude Code`
- The marker is cleared once the upgrade skill runs (skill calls `nurge upgrade-skills --done`)

I'll write the spec doc now.

input.content:
# Auto-Update Design

## Overview

Add automatic update checking and self-update capability to the nurge CLI. Users get notified when a new version is available and can update in-place with `nurge update`.

## Components

### 1. Update Checker — `internal/update/check.go`

**Responsibility:** Check GitHub for newer releases, cache results, compare versions.

**Cache file:** `~/.nurge/update-check.json`
```json
{
  "latest_version": "v0.2.0",
  "download_url": "https://github.com/usenurge/nurge/releases/tag/v0.2.0",
  "checked_at": "2026-03-25T14:00:00Z"
}
```

**TTL:** 1 hour. Within that window, reads from cache with zero network calls.

**API endpoint:** `GET https://api.github.com/repos/usenurge/nurge/releases/latest`
- No auth required (public repo)
- Rate limit: 60 req/hr unauthenticated — plenty for hourly checks

**Semver comparison:**
- Strip `v` prefix from both versions
- Split on `.`, compare major/minor/patch numerically
- Skip check entirely when `buildVersion == "dev"` (local development builds)
- Prerelease tags (alpha/beta/rc) are ignored unless `--pre` is used

**Functions:**
- `CheckForUpdate(currentVersion string) (*UpdateInfo, error)` — reads cache or hits API, returns nil if up to date
- `readCache() (*CacheEntry, error)` — reads and validates cache file
- `writeCache(entry *CacheEntry) error` — writes cache file
- `isNewer(current, latest string) bool` — semver comparison

### 2. Async Integration in `Execute()` — `cmd/root.go`

**Where:** In `Execute()`, start a goroutine before `rootCmd.Execute()`, collect result after.

**Flow:**
1. Before command runs: spawn goroutine calling `update.CheckForUpdate(buildVersion)`
2. Goroutine has 2s timeout via `context.WithTimeout`
3. After command completes and output is flushed: check if goroutine returned a result
4. If update available: print `→ nurge {version} available. Run: nurge update` to stderr
5. If goroutine timed out or errored: silently skip

**Skip notification for:** `update`, `version` commands, and `dev` builds.

**Output goes to stderr** so it doesn't pollute `--format json` output that skills parse.

### 3. Self-Update — `internal/update/update.go`

**Responsibility:** Download and replace the running binary.

**Functions:**
- `SelfUpdate(targetVersion string, prerelease bool) error`

**Flow:**
1. Fetch release info from GitHub API (specific version or latest)
2. Detect `runtime.GOOS` and `runtime.GOARCH`
3. Find matching asset: `nurge_{version}_{os}_{arch}.tar.gz`
4. Download to temp directory
5. Extract archive, find `nurge` binary
6. Get current executable path via `os.Executable()` + `filepath.EvalSymlinks()`
7. Atomic replace: write to `{path}.new`, rename over original, remove old
8. If rename fails with permission error: return clear error suggesting `sudo nurge update`
9. Clean up temp files
10. Write pending skill upgrade marker (see §4)

**Archive naming convention** (matches .goreleaser.yaml):
`nurge_{version}_{os}_{arch}.tar.gz` where version has no `v` prefix in the archive name but the release tag does.

Note: goreleaser uses `nurge_{Version}_{Os}_{Arch}` template. The `Version` variable does NOT include the `v` prefix. So for release `v0.2.0`, the archive is `nurge_0.2.0_darwin_arm64.tar.gz`.

### 4. `nurge update` Command — `cmd/update.go`

```
nurge update          # Update to latest stable release
nurge update --pre    # Include prereleases
nurge update v0.2.0   # Update to specific version
```

**Output:**
```
→ Downloading nurge v0.2.0 (darwin/arm64)...
✓ Updated nurge to v0.2.0 (was v0.1.0)
→ Skill templates updated. Run /nurge:upgrade-skills in Claude Code to apply.
```

**Flags:**
- `--pre` — include prerelease versions (alpha, beta, rc)

**Args:**
- Optional version string (e.g., `v0.2.0`) to pin a specific version

**Annotations:** `skipAuth: true` — update should work even if not logged in.

### 5. Post-Update Skill Migration Marker

**After successful self-update**, write `.nurge/pending-skill-upgrade.json`:
```json
{
  "from_version": "v0.1.0",
  "to_version": "v0.2.0",
  "updated_at": "2026-03-25T14:00:00Z"
}
```

**On next nurge command** (in the PersistentPreRunE or Execute path), if this file exists:
- Print: `→ Skill templates updated. Run /nurge:upgrade-skills in Claude Code to apply.`
- Do NOT auto-delete the marker — it's cleared when the upgrade skill runs `nurge upgrade-skills --done`

**`nurge upgrade-skills --done`** — hidden subcommand that deletes the marker file. Called by the Claude Code skill after it finishes regenerating skills.

### 6. Global Cache Directory

The update cache lives in `~/.nurge/` (user home, not project dir) because:
- Update checks are per-user, not per-project
- Works before `nurge init` (no `.nurge/` project dir yet)
- The `update` command itself needs no project context

Create `~/.nurge/` if it doesn't exist during cache write.

The pending-skill-upgrade marker lives in `.nurge/` (project dir) because it's per-project — each project's skills need independent upgrading.

## File Structure

```
internal/update/
  check.go     — CheckForUpdate, cache read/write, semver compare, UpdateInfo struct
  update.go    — SelfUpdate (download, extract, replace binary)
cmd/
  update.go    — cobra command for `nurge update`
  root.go      — (modified) async check goroutine in Execute()
```

## Edge Cases

- **Permission denied on replace:** Return actionable error: `permission denied: run sudo nurge update`
- **GitHub API down/rate limited:** Timeout after 2s, silently skip. No error shown to user.
- **No internet:** Same as above — timeout, skip.
- **Binary installed via homebrew/other:** `os.Executable()` resolves symlinks, so it replaces the actual binary. If that's in a managed location, the permission error path handles it.
- **dev builds:** Skip all update checks. `buildVersion == "dev"` is the signal.
- **Downgrade attempt:** `nurge update v0.1.0` when current is v0.2.0 — allow it. User knows what they're doing.
- **Same version:** Print `✓ Already on latest version (v0.2.0)` and exit.
- **Concurrent updates:** Atomic rename prevents corruption. Worst case: two processes both update successfully.

## Not In Scope

- Background daemon or scheduled checks
- Rollback (user can `nurge update v{old}` to pin)
- Changelog display (keep it simple, user can check GitHub)
- The `/nurge:upgrade-skills` skill itself (separate work)

    37→- `writeCache(entry *CacheEntry) error` — writes cache file
    38→- `isNewer(current, latest string) bool` — semver comparison
    39→
    40→### 2. Async Integration in `Execute()` — `cmd/root.go`
    41→
    42→**Where:** In `Execute()`, start a goroutine before `rootCmd.Execute()`, collect result after.
    43→
    44→**Flow:**
    45→1. Before command runs: spawn goroutine calling `update.CheckForUpdate(buildVersion)`
    46→2. Goroutine has 2s timeout via `context.WithTimeout`
    47→3. After command completes and output is flushed: check if goroutine returned a result
    48→4. If update available: print `→ nurge {version} available. Run: nurge update` to stderr
    49→5. If goroutine timed out or errored: silently skip
    50→
    51→**Skip notification for:** `update`, `version` commands, and `dev` builds.
    52→
    53→**Output goes to stderr** so it doesn't pollute `--format json` output that skills parse.
    54→
    55→### 3. Self-Update — `internal/update/update.go`
    56→
    57→**Responsibility:** Download and replace the running binary.
    58→
    59→**Functions:**
    60→- `SelfUpdate(targetVersion string, prerelease bool) error`
    61→
    62→**Flow:**
    63→1. Fetch release info from GitHub API (specific version or latest)
    64→2. Detect `runtime.GOOS` and `runtime.GOARCH`
    65→3. Find matching asset: `nurge_{version}_{os}_{arch}.tar.gz`
    66→4. Download to temp directory
    67→5. Extract archive, find `nurge` binary
    68→6. Get current executable path via `os.Executable()` + `filepath.EvalSymlinks()`
    69→7. Atomic replace: write to `{path}.new`, rename over original, remove old
    70→8. If rename fails with permission error: return clear error suggesting `sudo nurge update`
    71→9. Clean up temp files
    72→10. Write pending skill upgrade marker (see §4)
    73→
    74→**Archive naming convention** (matches .goreleaser.yaml):
    75→`nurge_{version}_{os}_{arch}.tar.gz` where version has no `v` prefix in the archive name but the release tag does.
    76→
    77→Note: goreleaser uses `nurge_{Version}_{Os}_{Arch}` template. The `Version` variable does NOT include the `v` prefix. So for release `v0.2.0`, the archive is `nurge_0.2.0_darwin_arm64.tar.gz`.
    78→
    79→### 4. `nurge update` Command — `cmd/update.go`
    80→
    81→```
    82→nurge update          # Update to latest stable release
    83→nurge update --pre    # Include prereleases
    84→nurge update v0.2.0   # Update to specific version
    85→```
    86→
    87→**Output:**
    88→```
    89→→ Downloading nurge v0.2.0 (darwin/arm64)...
    90→✓ Updated nurge to v0.2.0 (was v0.1.0)
    91→→ Skill templates updated. Run /nurge:upgrade-skills in Claude Code to apply.
    92→```
    93→
    94→**Flags:**
    95→- `--pre` — include prerelease versions (alpha, beta, rc)
    96→
    97→**Args:**
    98→- Optional version string (e.g., `v0.2.0`) to pin a specific version
    99→
   100→**Annotations:** `skipAuth: true` — update should work even if not logged in.
   101→
   102→### 5. Post-Update Skill Migration Marker
   103→
   104→**After successful self-update**, write `.nurge/pending-skill-upgrade.json`:
   105→```json
   106→{
   107→  "from_version": "v0.1.0",
   108→  "to_version": "v0.2.0",
   109→  "updated_at": "2026-03-25T14:00:00Z"
   110→}
   111→```
   112→
   113→**On next nurge command** (in the PersistentPreRunE or Execute path), if this file exists:
   114→- Print: `→ Skill templates updated. Run /nurge:upgrade-skills in Claude Code to apply.`
   115→- Do NOT auto-delete the marker — it's cleared when the upgrade skill runs `nurge upgrade-skills --done`
   116→
   117→**`nurge upgrade-skills --done`** — hidden subcommand that deletes the marker file. Called by the Claude Code skill after it finishes regenerating skills.
   118→
   119→### 6. Global Cache Directory
   120→
   121→The update cache lives in `~/.nurge/` (user home, not project dir) because:
   122→- Update checks are per-user, not per-project
   123→- Works before `nurge init` (no `.nurge/` project dir yet)
   124→- The `update` command itself needs no project context
   125→
   126→Create `~/.nurge/` if it doesn't exist during cache write.
   127→
   128→The pending-skill-upgrade marker lives in `.nurge/` (project dir) because it's per-project — each project's skills need independent upgrading.
   129→
   130→## File Structure
   131→
   132→```
   133→internal/update/
   134→  check.go     — CheckForUpdate, cache read/write, semver compare, UpdateInfo struct
   135→  update.go    — SelfUpdate (download, extract, replace binary)
   136→cmd/
   137→  update.go    — cobra command for `nurge update`
   138→  root.go      — (modified) async check goroutine in Execute()
   139→```
   140→
   141→## Edge Cases
   142→
   143→- **Permission denied on replace:** Return actionable error: `permission denied: run sudo nurge update`
   144→- **GitHub API down/rate limited:** Timeout after 2s, silently skip. No error shown to user.
   145→- **No internet:** Same as above — timeout, skip.
   146→- **Binary installed via homebrew/other:** `os.Executable()` resolves symlinks, so it replaces the actual binary. If that's in a managed location, the permission error path handles it.
   147→- **dev builds:** Skip all update checks. `buildVersion == "dev"` is the signal.
   148→- **Downgrade attempt:** `nurge update v0.1.0` when current is v0.2.0 — allow it. User knows what they're doing.
   149→- **Same version:** Print `✓ Already on latest version (v0.2.0)` and exit.
   150→- **Concurrent updates:** Atomic rename prevents corruption. Worst case: two processes both update successfully.
   151→
   152→## Not In Scope
   153→
   154→- Background daemon or scheduled checks
   155→- Rollback (user can `nurge update v{old}` to pin)
   156→- Changelog display (keep it simple, user can check GitHub)
   157→- The `/nurge:upgrade-skills` skill itself (separate work)
   158→

1→#!/bin/bash
     2→set -euo pipefail
     3→
     4→# Nurge installer
     5→# Usage:
     6→#   curl -fsSL https://install.nurge.sh | bash
     7→#   curl -fsSL https://install.nurge.sh | bash -s -- --pre
     8→#   curl -fsSL https://install.nurge.sh | bash -s -- v0.1.0
     9→
    10→REPO="usenurge/nurge"
    11→INSTALL_DIR="/usr/local/bin"
    12→BINARY="nurge"
    13→
    14→# --- Helpers ---
    15→
    16→info()  { printf "\033[1;34m→\033[0m %s\n" "$1"; }
    17→ok()    { printf "\033[1;32m✓\033[0m %s\n" "$1"; }
    18→warn()  { printf "\033[1;33m!\033[0m %s\n" "$1"; }
    19→fail()  { printf "\033[1;31m✗\033[0m %s\n" "$1" >&2; exit 1; }
    20→
    21→# --- Detect platform ---
    22→
    23→detect_os() {
    24→  case "$(uname -s)" in
    25→    Linux*)  echo "linux" ;;
    26→    Darwin*) echo "darwin" ;;
    27→    *)       fail "Unsupported OS: $(uname -s)" ;;
    28→  esac
    29→}
    30→
    31→detect_arch() {
    32→  case "$(uname -m)" in
    33→    x86_64|amd64)  echo "amd64" ;;
    34→    arm64|aarch64) echo "arm64" ;;
    35→    *)             fail "Unsupported architecture: $(uname -m)" ;;
    36→  esac
    37→}
    38→
    39→# --- Resolve version ---
    40→
    41→resolve_version() {
    42→  local requested="${1:-}"
    43→  local pre="${2:-false}"
    44→
    45→  if [ -n "$requested" ] && [ "$requested" != "--pre" ]; then
    46→    echo "$requested"
    47→    return
    48→  fi
    49→
    50→  info "Fetching latest release..."
    51→  local releases
    52→  releases=$(curl -fsSL "https://api.github.com/repos/${REPO}/releases?per_page=20") || fail "Failed to fetch releases"
    53→
    54→  local version=""
    55→  if [ "$pre" = "true" ]; then
    56→    version=$(echo "$releases" | grep -o '"tag_name": *"[^"]*"' | head -1 | sed 's/.*"tag_name": *"//;s/"//')
    57→  else
    58→    version=$(echo "$releases" | grep -o '"tag_name": *"[^"]*"' | grep -v 'alpha\|beta\|rc' | head -1 | sed 's/.*"tag_name": *"//;s/"//')
    59→  fi
    60→
    61→  [ -z "$version" ] && fail "No release found"
    62→  echo "$version"
    63→}
    64→
    65→# --- Download & install ---
    66→
    67→install() {
    68→  local version="$1"
    69→  local os="$2"
    70→  local arch="$3"
    71→
    72→  local archive_name="${BINARY}_${os}_${arch}"
    73→  local ext="tar.gz"
    74→  [ "$os" = "windows" ] && ext="zip"
    75→
    76→  local url="https://github.com/${REPO}/releases/download/${version}/${archive_name}.${ext}"
    77→
    78→  info "Downloading ${BINARY} ${version} (${os}/${arch})..."
    79→  local tmpdir
    80→  tmpdir=$(mktemp -d)
    81→  trap 'rm -rf "$tmpdir"' EXIT
    82→
    83→  curl -fsSL "$url" -o "${tmpdir}/archive.${ext}" || fail "Download failed. Check that ${version} exists at https://github.com/${REPO}/releases"
    84→
    85→  info "Extracting..."
    86→  if [ "$ext" = "tar.gz" ]; then
    87→    tar -xzf "${tmpdir}/archive.${ext}" -C "$tmpdir"
    88→  else
    89→    unzip -q "${tmpdir}/archive.${ext}" -d "$tmpdir"
    90→  fi
    91→
    92→  # Find the binary (may be nested in a directory)
    93→  local bin_path
    94→  bin_path=$(find "$tmpdir" -name "$BINARY" -type f | head -1)
    95→  [ -z "$bin_path" ] && fail "Binary not found in archive"
    96→
    97→  chmod +x "$bin_path"
    98→
    99→  # Install to target directory
   100→  if [ -w "$INSTALL_DIR" ]; then
   101→    mv "$bin_path" "${INSTALL_DIR}/${BINARY}"
   102→  else
   103→    info "Need sudo to install to ${INSTALL_DIR}"
   104→    sudo mv "$bin_path" "${INSTALL_DIR}/${BINARY}"
   105→  fi
   106→
   107→  ok "Installed ${BINARY} ${version} to ${INSTALL_DIR}/${BINARY}"
   108→}
   109→
   110→# --- Main ---
   111→
   112→main() {
   113→  local arg="${1:-}"
   114→  local pre=false
   115→
   116→  if [ "$arg" = "--pre" ]; then
   117→    pre=true
   118→    arg=""
   119→  fi
   120→
   121→  local os arch version
   122→  os=$(detect_os)
   123→  arch=$(detect_arch)
   124→  version=$(resolve_version "$arg" "$pre")
   125→
   126→  install "$version" "$os" "$arch"
   127→
   128→  # Post-install: next steps
   129→  echo ""
   130→  echo "  Get started:"
   131→  echo "    nurge my-outbound"
   132→  echo "    cd my-outbound"
   133→  echo ""
   134→}
   135→
   136→main "$@"
   137→

This is very revealing. The `install.sh` uses a different archive naming convention than what the spec describes. Let me verify the exact archive name pattern from the goreleaser config vs the install script vs the spec.

Now I have all the information needed for a thorough review. Let me compile my findings.

---

## Design Spec Review: Auto-Update (`2026-03-25-auto-update-design.md`)

### What's Done Well

The spec is well-structured, clearly scoped, and thoughtfully designed. The separation into update checker, self-update logic, and cobra command is clean. Several good decisions stand out:

- Async check with 2-second timeout so it never blocks the CLI
- Output to stderr to avoid polluting `--format json` output
- Cache with TTL to avoid hammering the GitHub API
- Skipping checks for dev builds and the `update`/`version` commands themselves
- Splitting the cache (global `~/.nurge/`) from the skill migration marker (project `.nurge/`)
- The `skipAuth: true` annotation, consistent with how `account` and `version` commands work

---

### CRITICAL Issues (Must Fix)

**1. Archive naming mismatch between spec and install.sh**

The spec says (line 75-77):
> `nurge_{version}_{os}_{arch}.tar.gz` where version has no `v` prefix

This matches `.goreleaser.yaml` line 24:
```
name_template: "nurge_{{ .Version }}_{{ .Os }}_{{ .Arch }}"
```

However, the `install.sh` (line 72) uses a **different** format:
```bash
local archive_name="${BINARY}_${os}_${arch}"
```

That produces `nurge_darwin_arm64.tar.gz` -- **no version component at all**. This means one of two things: either `install.sh` is broken/outdated, or the GoReleaser config was changed after `install.sh` was written.

Since the goreleaser config is the source of truth for what actually gets published, the spec's archive naming (`nurge_{Version}_{Os}_{Arch}.tar.gz`) is correct. But you should fix `install.sh` to match, otherwise the install script is also broken for the same releases the updater would target. If `install.sh` currently works, that would mean the goreleaser template was recently changed, and the archives in existing releases might not match what the spec expects.

**Action:** Verify what the actual published release assets look like on GitHub. If they match the goreleaser template (`nurge_0.2.0_darwin_arm64.tar.gz`), then `install.sh` is buggy. If they match `install.sh` (`nurge_darwin_arm64.tar.gz`), then the goreleaser config was recently changed and hasn't been released yet.

**2. Windows archive format not handled**

The spec says "collect result after" the command runs, but doesn't specify the mechanism. The idiomatic Go approach would be a channel:

```go
type updateResult struct {
    info *update.UpdateInfo
    err  error
}
ch := make(chan updateResult, 1)
go func() {
    info, err := update.CheckForUpdate(buildVersion)
    ch <- updateResult{info, err}

1→# Auto-Update Design
     2→
     3→## Overview
     4→
     5→Add automatic update checking and self-update capability to the nurge CLI. Users get notified when a new version is available and can update in-place with `nurge update`.
     6→
     7→## Components
     8→
     9→### 1. Update Checker — `internal/update/check.go`
    10→
    11→**Responsibility:** Check GitHub for newer releases, cache results, compare versions.
    12→
    13→**Cache file:** `~/.nurge/update-check.json`
    14→```json
    15→{
    16→  "latest_version": "v0.2.0",
    17→  "download_url": "https://github.com/usenurge/nurge/releases/tag/v0.2.0",
    18→  "checked_at": "2026-03-25T14:00:00Z"
    19→}
    20→```
    21→
    22→**TTL:** 1 hour. Within that window, reads from cache with zero network calls.
    23→
    24→**API endpoint:** `GET https://api.github.com/repos/usenurge/nurge/releases/latest`
    25→- No auth required (public repo)
    26→- Rate limit: 60 req/hr unauthenticated — plenty for hourly checks
    27→
    28→**Semver comparison:**
    29→- Strip `v` prefix from both versions
    30→- Split on `.`, compare major/minor/patch numerically
    31→- Skip check entirely when `buildVersion == "dev"` (local development builds)
    32→- Prerelease tags (alpha/beta/rc) are ignored unless `--pre` is used
    33→
    34→**Functions:**
    35→- `CheckForUpdate(currentVersion string) (*UpdateInfo, error)` — reads cache or hits API, returns nil if up to date
    36→- `readCache() (*CacheEntry, error)` — reads and validates cache file
    37→- `writeCache(entry *CacheEntry) error` — writes cache file
    38→- `isNewer(current, latest string) bool` — semver comparison
    39→
    40→### 2. Async Integration in `Execute()` — `cmd/root.go`
    41→
    42→**Where:** In `Execute()`, start a goroutine before `rootCmd.Execute()`, collect result after.
    43→
    44→**Flow:**
    45→1. Before command runs: spawn goroutine calling `update.CheckForUpdate(buildVersion)`
    46→2. Goroutine has 2s timeout via `context.WithTimeout`
    47→3. After command completes and output is flushed: check if goroutine returned a result
    48→4. If update available: print `→ nurge {version} available. Run: nurge update` to stderr
    49→5. If goroutine timed out or errored: silently skip
    50→
    51→**Skip notification for:** `update`, `version` commands, and `dev` builds.
    52→
    53→**Output goes to stderr** so it doesn't pollute `--format json` output that skills parse.
    54→
    55→### 3. Self-Update — `internal/update/update.go`
    56→
    57→**Responsibility:** Download and replace the running binary.
    58→
    59→**Functions:**
    60→- `SelfUpdate(targetVersion string, prerelease bool) error`
    61→
    62→**Flow:**
    63→1. Fetch release info from GitHub API (specific version or latest)
    64→2. Detect `runtime.GOOS` and `runtime.GOARCH`
    65→3. Find matching asset: `nurge_{version}_{os}_{arch}.tar.gz`
    66→4. Download to temp directory
    67→5. Extract archive, find `nurge` binary
    68→6. Get current executable path via `os.Executable()` + `filepath.EvalSymlinks()`
    69→7. Atomic replace: write to `{path}.new`, rename over original, remove old
    70→8. If rename fails with permission error: return clear error suggesting `sudo nurge update`
    71→9. Clean up temp files
    72→10. Write pending skill upgrade marker (see §4)
    73→
    74→**Archive naming convention** (matches .goreleaser.yaml):
    75→`nurge_{version}_{os}_{arch}.tar.gz` where version has no `v` prefix in the archive name but the release tag does.
    76→
    77→Note: goreleaser uses `nurge_{Version}_{Os}_{Arch}` template. The `Version` variable does NOT include the `v` prefix. So for release `v0.2.0`, the archive is `nurge_0.2.0_darwin_arm64.tar.gz`.
    78→
    79→### 4. `nurge update` Command — `cmd/update.go`
    80→
    81→```
    82→nurge update          # Update to latest stable release
    83→nurge update --pre    # Include prereleases
    84→nurge update v0.2.0   # Update to specific version
    85→```
    86→
    87→**Output:**
    88→```
    89→→ Downloading nurge v0.2.0 (darwin/arm64)...
    90→✓ Updated nurge to v0.2.0 (was v0.1.0)
    91→→ Skill templates updated. Run /nurge:upgrade-skills in Claude Code to apply.
    92→```
    93→
    94→**Flags:**
    95→- `--pre` — include prerelease versions (alpha, beta, rc)
    96→
    97→**Args:**
    98→- Optional version string (e.g., `v0.2.0`) to pin a specific version
    99→
   100→**Annotations:** `skipAuth: true` — update should work even if not logged in.
   101→
   102→### 5. Post-Update Skill Migration Marker
   103→
   104→**After successful self-update**, write `.nurge/pending-skill-upgrade.json`:
   105→```json
   106→{
   107→  "from_version": "v0.1.0",
   108→  "to_version": "v0.2.0",
   109→  "updated_at": "2026-03-25T14:00:00Z"
   110→}
   111→```
   112→
   113→**On next nurge command** (in the PersistentPreRunE or Execute path), if this file exists:
   114→- Print: `→ Skill templates updated. Run /nurge:upgrade-skills in Claude Code to apply.`
   115→- Do NOT auto-delete the marker — it's cleared when the upgrade skill runs `nurge upgrade-skills --done`
   116→
   117→**`nurge upgrade-skills --done`** — hidden subcommand that deletes the marker file. Called by the Claude Code skill after it finishes regenerating skills.
   118→
   119→### 6. Global Cache Directory
   120→
   121→The update cache lives in `~/.nurge/` (user home, not project dir) because:
   122→- Update checks are per-user, not per-project
   123→- Works before `nurge init` (no `.nurge/` project dir yet)
   124→- The `update` command itself needs no project context
   125→
   126→Create `~/.nurge/` if it doesn't exist during cache write.
   127→
   128→The pending-skill-upgrade marker lives in `.nurge/` (project dir) because it's per-project — each project's skills need independent upgrading.
   129→
   130→## File Structure
   131→
   132→```
   133→internal/update/
   134→  check.go     — CheckForUpdate, cache read/write, semver compare, UpdateInfo struct
   135→  update.go    — SelfUpdate (download, extract, replace binary)
   136→cmd/
   137→  update.go    — cobra command for `nurge update`
   138→  root.go      — (modified) async check goroutine in Execute()
   139→```
   140→
   141→## Edge Cases
   142→
   143→- **Permission denied on replace:** Return actionable error: `permission denied: run sudo nurge update`
   144→- **GitHub API down/rate limited:** Timeout after 2s, silently skip. No error shown to user.
   145→- **No internet:** Same as above — timeout, skip.
   146→- **Binary installed via homebrew/other:** `os.Executable()` resolves symlinks, so it replaces the actual binary. If that's in a managed location, the permission error path handles it.

# Auto-Update Design

## Overview

Add automatic update checking and self-update capability to the nurge CLI. Users get notified when a new version is available and can update in-place with `nurge update`.

## Components

### 1. Update Checker — `internal/update/check.go`

**Responsibility:** Check GitHub for newer releases, cache results, compare versions.

**Cache file:** `~/.nurge/update-check.json`
```json
{
  "latest_version": "v0.2.0",
  "download_url": "https://github.com/usenurge/nurge/releases/tag/v0.2.0",
  "checked_at": "2026-03-25T14:00:00Z"
}
```

**TTL:** 1 hour. Within that window, reads from cache with zero network calls.

**API endpoint:** `GET https://api.github.com/repos/usenurge/nurge/releases/latest`
- No auth required (public repo)
- Rate limit: 60 req/hr unauthenticated — plenty for hourly checks

**Semver comparison:**
- Strip `v` prefix from both versions
- Split on `.`, compare major/minor/patch numerically
- Skip check entirely when `buildVersion == "dev"` (local development builds)
- Prerelease tags (alpha/beta/rc) are ignored unless `--pre` is used

**Functions:**
- `CheckForUpdate(currentVersion string) (*UpdateInfo, error)` — reads cache or hits API, returns nil if up to date
- `readCache() (*CacheEntry, error)` — reads and validates cache file
- `writeCache(entry *CacheEntry) error` — writes cache file
- `isNewer(current, latest string) bool` — semver comparison

### 2. Async Integration in `Execute()` — `cmd/root.go`

**Where:** In `Execute()`, start a goroutine before `rootCmd.Execute()`, collect result after.

**Flow:**
1. Before command runs: spawn goroutine calling `update.CheckForUpdate(buildVersion)`
2. Goroutine has 2s timeout via `context.WithTimeout`
3. After command completes and output is flushed: check if goroutine returned a result
4. If update available: print `→ nurge {version} available. Run: nurge update` to stderr
5. If goroutine timed out or errored: silently skip

**Skip notification for:** `update`, `version` commands, and `dev` builds.

**Output goes to stderr** so it doesn't pollute `--format json` output that skills parse.

### 3. Self-Update — `internal/update/update.go`

**Responsibility:** Download and replace the running binary.

**Functions:**
- `SelfUpdate(targetVersion string, prerelease bool) error`

**Flow:**
1. Fetch release info from GitHub API (specific version or latest)
2. Detect `runtime.GOOS` and `runtime.GOARCH`
3. Find matching asset: `nurge_{version}_{os}_{arch}.tar.gz`
4. Download to temp directory
5. Extract archive, find `nurge` binary
6. Get current executable path via `os.Executable()` + `filepath.EvalSymlinks()`
7. Atomic replace: write to `{path}.new`, rename over original, remove old
8. If rename fails with permission error: return clear error suggesting `sudo nurge update`
9. Clean up temp files
10. Write pending skill upgrade marker (see §4)

**Archive naming convention** (matches .goreleaser.yaml):
`nurge_{version}_{os}_{arch}.tar.gz` where version has no `v` prefix in the archive name but the release tag does.

Note: goreleaser uses `nurge_{Version}_{Os}_{Arch}` template. The `Version` variable does NOT include the `v` prefix. So for release `v0.2.0`, the archive is `nurge_0.2.0_darwin_arm64.tar.gz`.

### 4. `nurge update` Command — `cmd/update.go`

```
nurge update          # Update to latest stable release
nurge update --pre    # Include prereleases
nurge update v0.2.0   # Update to specific version
```

**Output:**
```
→ Downloading nurge v0.2.0 (darwin/arm64)...
✓ Updated nurge to v0.2.0 (was v0.1.0)
→ Skill templates updated. Run /nurge:upgrade-skills in Claude Code to apply.
```

**Flags:**
- `--pre` — include prerelease versions (alpha, beta, rc)

**Args:**
- Optional version string (e.g., `v0.2.0`) to pin a specific version

**Annotations:** `skipAuth: true` — update should work even if not logged in.

### 5. Post-Update Skill Migration Marker

**After successful self-update**, write `.nurge/pending-skill-upgrade.json`:
```json
{
  "from_version": "v0.1.0",
  "to_version": "v0.2.0",
  "updated_at": "2026-03-25T14:00:00Z"
}
```

**On next nurge command** (in the PersistentPreRunE or Execute path), if this file exists:
- Print: `→ Skill templates updated. Run /nurge:upgrade-skills in Claude Code to apply.`
- Do NOT auto-delete the marker — it's cleared when the upgrade skill runs `nurge upgrade-skills --done`

**`nurge upgrade-skills --done`** — hidden subcommand that deletes the marker file. Called by the Claude Code skill after it finishes regenerating skills.

### 6. Global Cache Directory

The update cache lives in `~/.nurge/` (user home, not project dir) because:
- Update checks are per-user, not per-project
- Works before `nurge init` (no `.nurge/` project dir yet)
- The `update` command itself needs no project context

Create `~/.nurge/` if it doesn't exist during cache write.

The pending-skill-upgrade marker lives in `.nurge/` (project dir) because it's per-project — each project's skills need independent upgrading.

## File Structure

```
internal/update/
  check.go     — CheckForUpdate, cache read/write, semver compare, UpdateInfo struct
  update.go    — SelfUpdate (download, extract, replace binary)
cmd/
  update.go    — cobra command for `nurge update`
  root.go      — (modified) async check goroutine in Execute()
```

## Edge Cases

- **Permission denied on replace:** Return actionable error: `permission denied: run sudo nurge update`
- **GitHub API down/rate limited:** Timeout after 2s, silently skip. No error shown to user.
- **No internet:** Same as above — timeout, skip.
- **Binary installed via homebrew/other:** `os.Executable()` resolves symlinks, so it replaces the actual binary. If that's in a managed location, the permission error path handles it.
- **dev builds:** Skip all update checks. `buildVersion == "dev"` is the signal.
- **Downgrade attempt:** `nurge update v0.1.0` when current is v0.2.0 — allow it. User knows what they're doing.
- **Same version:** Print `✓ Already on latest version (v0.2.0)` and exit.
- **Concurrent updates:** Atomic rename prevents corruption. Worst case: two processes both update successfully.

## Not In Scope

- Background daemon or scheduled checks
- Rollback (user can `nurge update v{old}` to pin)
- Changelog display (keep it simple, user can check GitHub)
- The `/nurge:upgrade-skills` skill itself (separate work)

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Auto-Update Design

## Overview

Add automatic update checking and self-update capability to the nurge CLI. Users get notified when a new version is available and can update in-place with `nurge update`.

## Components

### 1. Update Checker — `internal/update/check.go`

**Responsibility:** Check GitHub for newer releases, cache results, compare versions.

**Cache file:** `~/.nurge/update-check.json`
```json
{
  "latest_version": "v0.2.0",
  "download_url": "https://github.com/usenurge/nurge/releases/tag/v0.2.0",
  "checked_at": "2026-03-25T14:00:00Z"
}
```

**TTL:** 1 hour. Within that window, reads from cache with zero network calls.

**API endpoint:** `GET https://api.github.com/repos/usenurge/nurge/releases/latest`
- No auth required (public repo)
- Rate limit: 60 req/hr unauthenticated — plenty for hourly checks

**Semver comparison:**
- Strip `v` prefix from both versions
- Split on `.`, compare major/minor/patch numerically
- Skip check entirely when `buildVersion == "dev"` (local development builds)
- Prerelease tags (alpha/beta/rc) are ignored unless `--pre` is used

**Functions:**
- `CheckForUpdate(currentVersion string) (*UpdateInfo, error)` — reads cache or hits API, returns nil if up to date
- `readCache() (*CacheEntry, error)` — reads and validates cache file
- `writeCache(entry *CacheEntry) error` — writes cache file
- `isNewer(current, latest string) bool` — semver comparison

### 2. Async Integration in `Execute()` — `cmd/root.go`

**Where:** In `Execute()`, start a goroutine before `rootCmd.Execute()`, collect result after.

**Flow:**
1. Before command runs: spawn goroutine calling `update.CheckForUpdate(buildVersion)`
2. Goroutine has 2s timeout via `context.WithTimeout`
3. After command completes and output is flushed: check if goroutine returned a result
4. If update available: print `→ nurge {version} available. Run: nurge update` to stderr
5. If goroutine timed out or errored: silently skip

**Skip notification for:** `update`, `version` commands, and `dev` builds.

**Output goes to stderr** so it doesn't pollute `--format json` output that skills parse.

### 3. Self-Update — `internal/update/update.go`

**Responsibility:** Download and replace the running binary.

**Functions:**
- `SelfUpdate(targetVersion string, prerelease bool) error`

**Flow:**
1. Fetch release info from GitHub API (specific version or latest)
2. Detect `runtime.GOOS` and `runtime.GOARCH`
3. Find matching asset: `nurge_{version}_{os}_{arch}.tar.gz`
4. Download to temp directory
5. Extract archive, find `nurge` binary
6. Get current executable path via `os.Executable()` + `filepath.EvalSymlinks()`
7. Atomic replace: write to `{path}.new`, rename over original, remove old
8. If rename fails with permission error: return clear error suggesting `sudo nurge update`
9. Clean up temp files
10. Write pending skill upgrade marker (see §4)

**Archive naming convention** (matches .goreleaser.yaml):
`nurge_{version}_{os}_{arch}.tar.gz` where version has no `v` prefix in the archive name but the release tag does.

Note: goreleaser uses `nurge_{Version}_{Os}_{Arch}` template. The `Version` variable does NOT include the `v` prefix. So for release `v0.2.0`, the archive is `nurge_0.2.0_darwin_arm64.tar.gz`.

### 4. `nurge update` Command — `cmd/update.go`

```
nurge update          # Update to latest stable release
nurge update --pre    # Include prereleases
nurge update v0.2.0   # Update to specific version
```

**Output:**
```
→ Downloading nurge v0.2.0 (darwin/arm64)...
✓ Updated nurge to v0.2.0 (was v0.1.0)
→ Skill templates updated. Run /nurge:upgrade-skills in Claude Code to apply.
```

**Flags:**
- `--pre` — include prerelease versions (alpha, beta, rc)

**Args:**
- Optional version string (e.g., `v0.2.0`) to pin a specific version

**Annotations:** `skipAuth: true` — update should work even if not logged in.

### 5. Post-Update Skill Migration Marker

**After successful self-update**, write `.nurge/pending-skill-upgrade.json`:
```json
{
  "from_version": "v0.1.0",
  "to_version": "v0.2.0",
  "updated_at": "2026-03-25T14:00:00Z"
}
```

**On next nurge command** (in the PersistentPreRunE or Execute path), if this file exists:
- Print: `→ Skill templates updated. Run /nurge:upgrade-skills in Claude Code to apply.`
- Do NOT auto-delete the marker — it's cleared when the upgrade skill runs `nurge upgrade-skills --done`

**`nurge upgrade-skills --done`** — hidden subcommand that deletes the marker file. Called by the Claude Code skill after it finishes regenerating skills.

### 6. Global Cache Directory

The update cache lives in `~/.nurge/` (user home, not project dir) because:
- Update checks are per-user, not per-project
- Works before `nurge init` (no `.nurge/` project dir yet)
- The `update` command itself needs no project context

Create `~/.nurge/` if it doesn't exist during cache write.

The pending-skill-upgrade marker lives in `.nurge/` (project dir) because it's per-project — each project's skills need independent upgrading.

## File Structure

```
internal/update/
  check.go     — CheckForUpdate, cache read/write, semver compare, UpdateInfo struct
  update.go    — SelfUpdate (download, extract, replace binary)
cmd/
  update.go    — cobra command for `nurge update`
  root.go      — (modified) async check goroutine in Execute()
```

## Edge Cases

- **Permission denied on replace:** Return actionable error: `permission denied: run sudo nurge update`
- **GitHub API down/rate limited:** Timeout after 2s, silently skip. No error shown to user.
- **No internet:** Same as above — timeout, skip.
- **Binary installed via homebrew/other:** `os.Executable()` resolves symlinks, so it replaces the actual binary. If that's in a managed location, the permission error path handles it.
- **dev builds:** Skip all update checks. `buildVersion == "dev"` is the signal.
- **Downgrade attempt:** `nurge update v0.1.0` when current is v0.2.0 — allow it. User knows what they're doing.
- **Same version:** Print `✓ Already on latest version (v0.2.0)` and exit.
- **Concurrent updates:** Atomic rename prevents corruption. Worst case: two processes both update successfully.

## Not In Scope

- Background daemon or scheduled checks
- Rollback (user can `nurge update v{old}` to pin)
- Changelog display (keep it simple, user can check GitHub)
- The `/nurge:upgrade-skills` skill itself (separate work)

# Brainstorming Ideas Into Designs

Help turn ideas into fully formed designs and specs through natural collaborative dialogue.

Start by understanding the current project context, then ask questions one at a time to refine the idea. Once you understand what you're building, present the design and get user approval.

<HARD-GATE>
Do NOT invoke any implementation skill, write any code, scaffold any project, or take any implementation action until you have presented a design and the user has approved it. This applies to EVERY project regardless of perceived simplicity.
</HARD-GATE>

## Anti-Pattern: "This Is Too Simple To Need A Design"

Every project goes through this process. A todo list, a single-function utility, a config change — all of them. "Simple" projects are where unexamined assumptions cause the most wasted work. The design can be short (a few sentences for truly simple projects), but you MUST present it and get approval.

## Checklist

You MUST create a task for each of these items and complete them in order:

1. **Explore project context** — check files, docs, recent commits
2. **Offer visual companion** (if topic will involve visual questions) — this is its own message, not combined with a clarifying question. See the Visual Companion section below.
3. **Ask clarifying questions** — one at a time, understand purpose/constraints/success criteria
4. **Propose 2-3 approaches** — with trade-offs and your recommendation
5. **Present design** — in sections scaled to their complexity, get user approval after each section
6. **Write design doc** — save to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md` and commit
7. **Spec review loop** — dispatch spec-document-reviewer subagent with precisely crafted review context (never your session history); fix issues and re-dispatch until approved (max 3 iterations, then surface to human)
8. **User reviews written spec** — ask user to review the spec file before proceeding
9. **Transition to implementation** — invoke writing-plans skill to create implementation plan

## Process Flow

```dot
digraph brainstorming {
    "Explore project context" [shape=box];
    "Visual questions ahead?" [shape=diamond];
    "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" [shape=box];
    "Ask clarifying questions" [shape=box];
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" [shape=box];
    "Present design sections" [shape=box];
    "User approves design?" [shape=diamond];
    "Write design doc" [shape=box];
    "Spec review loop" [shape=box];
    "Spec review passed?" [shape=diamond];
    "User reviews spec?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke writing-plans skill" [shape=doublecircle];

    "Explore project context" -> "Visual questions ahead?";
    "Visual questions ahead?" -> "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" [label="yes"];
    "Visual questions ahead?" -> "Ask clarifying questions" [label="no"];
    "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" -> "Ask clarifying questions";
    "Ask clarifying questions" -> "Propose 2-3 approaches";
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" -> "Present design sections";
    "Present design sections" -> "User approves design?";
    "User approves design?" -> "Present design sections" [label="no, revise"];
    "User approves design?" -> "Write design doc" [label="yes"];
    "Write design doc" -> "Spec review loop";
    "Spec review loop" -> "Spec review passed?";
    "Spec review passed?" -> "Spec review loop" [label="issues found,\nfix and re-dispatch"];
    "Spec review passed?" -> "User reviews spec?" [label="approved"];
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Write design doc" [label="changes requested"];
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Invoke writing-plans skill" [label="approved"];
}
```

**The terminal state is invoking writing-plans.** Do NOT invoke frontend-design, mcp-builder, or any other implementation skill. The ONLY skill you invoke after brainstorming is writing-plans.

## The Process

**Understanding the idea:**

- Check out the current project state first (files, docs, recent commits)
- Before asking detailed questions, assess scope: if the request describes multiple independent subsystems (e.g., "build a platform with chat, file storage, billing, and analytics"), flag this immediately. Don't spend questions refining details of a project that needs to be decomposed first.
- If the project is too large for a single spec, help the user decompose into sub-projects: what are the independent pieces, how do they relate, what order should they be built? Then brainstorm the first sub-project through the normal design flow. Each sub-project gets its own spec → plan → implementation cycle.
- For appropriately-scoped projects, ask questions one at a time to refine the idea
- Prefer multiple choice questions when possible, but open-ended is fine too
- Only one question per message - if a topic needs more exploration, break it into multiple questions
- Focus on understanding: purpose, constraints, success criteria

**Exploring approaches:**

- Propose 2-3 different approaches with trade-offs
- Present options conversationally with your recommendation and reasoning
- Lead with your recommended option and explain why

**Presenting the design:**

- Once you believe you understand what you're building, present the design
- Scale each section to its complexity: a few sentences if straightforward, up to 200-300 words if nuanced
- Ask after each section whether it looks right so far
- Cover: architecture, components, data flow, error handling, testing
- Be ready to go back and clarify if something doesn't make sense

**Design for isolation and clarity:**

- Break the system into smaller units that each have one clear purpose, communicate through well-defined interfaces, and can be understood and tested independently
- For each unit, you should be able to answer: what does it do, how do you use it, and what does it depend on?
- Can someone understand what a unit does without reading its internals? Can you change the internals without breaking consumers? If not, the boundaries need work.
- Smaller, well-bounded units are also easier for you to work with - you reason better about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more reliable when files are focused. When a file grows large, that's often a signal that it's doing too much.

**Working in existing codebases:**

- Explore the current structure before proposing changes. Follow existing patterns.
- Where existing code has problems that affect the work (e.g., a file that's grown too large, unclear boundaries, tangled responsibilities), include targeted improvements as part of the design - the way a good developer improves code they're working in.
- Don't propose unrelated refactoring. Stay focused on what serves the current goal.

## After the Design

**Documentation:**

- Write the validated design (spec) to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md`
  - (User preferences for spec location override this default)
- Use elements-of-style:writing-clearly-and-concisely skill if available
- Commit the design document to git

**Spec Review Loop:**
After writing the spec document:

1. Dispatch spec-document-reviewer subagent (see spec-document-reviewer-prompt.md)
2. If Issues Found: fix, re-dispatch, repeat until Approved
3. If loop exceeds 3 iterations, surface to human for guidance

**User Review Gate:**
After the spec review loop passes, ask the user to review the written spec before proceeding:

> "Spec written and committed to `<path>`. Please review it and let me know if you want to make any changes before we start writing out the implementation plan."

Wait for the user's response. If they request changes, make them and re-run the spec review loop. Only proceed once the user approves.

**Implementation:**

- Invoke the writing-plans skill to create a detailed implementation plan
- Do NOT invoke any other skill. writing-plans is the next step.

## Key Principles

- **One question at a time** - Don't overwhelm with multiple questions
- **Multiple choice preferred** - Easier to answer than open-ended when possible
- **YAGNI ruthlessly** - Remove unnecessary features from all designs
- **Explore alternatives** - Always propose 2-3 approaches before settling
- **Incremental validation** - Present design, get approval before moving on
- **Be flexible** - Go back and clarify when something doesn't make sense

## Visual Companion

A browser-based companion for showing mockups, diagrams, and visual options during brainstorming. Available as a tool — not a mode. Accepting the companion means it's available for questions that benefit from visual treatment; it does NOT mean every question goes through the browser.

**Offering the companion:** When you anticipate that upcoming questions will involve visual content (mockups, layouts, diagrams), offer it once for consent:
> "Some of what we're working on might be easier to explain if I can show it to you in a web browser. I can put together mockups, diagrams, comparisons, and other visuals as we go. This feature is still new and can be token-intensive. Want to try it? (Requires opening a local URL)"

**This offer MUST be its own message.** Do not combine it with clarifying questions, context summaries, or any other content. The message should contain ONLY the offer above and nothing else. Wait for the user's response before continuing. If they decline, proceed with text-only brainstorming.

**Per-question decision:** Even after the user accepts, decide FOR EACH QUESTION whether to use the browser or the terminal. The test: **would the user understand this better by seeing it than reading it?**

- **Use the browser** for content that IS visual — mockups, wireframes, layout comparisons, architecture diagrams, side-by-side visual designs
- **Use the terminal** for content that is text — requirements questions, conceptual choices, tradeoff lists, A/B/C/D text options, scope decisions

A question about a UI topic is not automatically a visual question. "What does personality mean in this context?" is a conceptual question — use the terminal. "Which wizard layout works better?" is a visual question — use the browser.

If they agree to the companion, read the detailed guide before proceeding:
`skills/brainstorming/visual-companion.md`
We already have the approved spec. Let me implement it directly.

content.text:
Done. Here's what was built:

**Files created:**
- `internal/update/check.go` — update checker with GitHub API, 1hr cache in `~/.nurge/`, semver compare
- `internal/update/update.go` — self-update: download, SHA256 checksum verify, extract tar.gz, atomic binary replace
- `cmd/update.go` — `nurge update [version] [--pre]` command (skipAuth)

**Files modified:**
- `cmd/root.go` — async goroutine in `Execute()` with 2s timeout + channel, stderr notification; pending upgrade marker check in `PersistentPreRunE`
- `cmd/skills.go` — hidden `nurge skills upgrade done` subcommand to clear the marker

**How it works:**
1. Every command (except `update`/`version`/dev builds) spawns a background goroutine that checks for updates
2. If a newer version exists, prints `→ nurge v0.x.0 available. Run: nurge update` to stderr after the command output
3. `nurge update` downloads the right archive, verifies checksum against `checksums.txt`, extracts, and replaces the binary in-place
4. After update, writes `.nurge/pending-skill-upgrade.json` — subsequent commands remind the user to run `/nurge:upgrade-skills`
